title: Get Financial data for data analysis
crumbs: Get Financial data for data analysis

title: Get Financial data for data analysis
text:
Step 1: Data extraction

Navigate to the SEC’s EDGAR database:

Microsoft

Tesla

Apple



Manual extraction:

For each company, find the 10-K filings for the last three fiscal years.

Extract the following financial figures: Total Revenue, Net Income, Total Assets, Total Liabilities, and Cash Flow from Operating Activities.



Organize Your Data:

Compile the extracted data into an Excel spreadsheet for easy reference during your Python analysis.
crumbs: Get Financial data for data analysis

title: Get Financial data for data analysis
section: Step 2: Preparing your Jupyter Notebook environment
text:
Step 2: Preparing your Jupyter Notebook environment
Install Jupyter (if not already installed):
Install Jupyter using pip if you haven’t already: > pip install notebook

Launch Jupyter Notebook: > jupyter notebook
This command should open Jupyter in your web browser.


Create a new notebook:
In the Jupyter interface, create a new notebook for your analysis.
crumbs: Get Financial data for data analysis

title: Get Financial data for data analysis
text:
Step 3: Python analysis in Jupyter

Import pandas:
At the beginning of your notebook, import the pandas library to work with your data. > import pandas as pd


Load your data:

Convert your Excel file to a CSV file for easier handling, then load it into a DataFrame. > df = pd.read_csv(‘path_to_your_csv_file.csv’)



Analyzing trends with pandas:

Use pandas to calculate year-over-year changes for each financial metric. You can do this by creating new columns in your DataFrame that represent the percentage change from one year to the next.


df[‘Revenue Growth (%)’] = df.groupby([‘Company’])[‘Total Revenue’].pct_change() * 100 df[‘Net Income Growth (%)’] = df.groupby([‘Company’])[‘Net Income’].pct_change() * 100


Explore other aggregate functions or groupings to analyze the data across different dimensions (by company, over years, etc.).



Summarizing findings:

Conclude your analysis by summarizing your findings directly in the notebook. Use markdown cells to add narrative explanations of your analysis, discussing the trends and changes in financial metrics you’ve identified.
crumbs: Get Financial data for data analysis

title: Get Financial data for data analysis
text:
Step 4: Documentation and submission

Document your analysis: Use the markdown feature in Jupyter Notebook to document your methodology, observations, and conclusions throughout the notebook.
Export your notebook: Once your analysis is complete, export your Jupyter Notebook as a PDF or HTML file for submission.

You can do this from the “File” menu in Jupyter, selecting “Download as” and then choosing your preferred format.


This approach allows you to focus on the core analytical aspects using pandas within a Jupyter Notebook, providing a clear, documented narrative of your financial analysis process. By the end of this task, you’ll have a comprehensive understanding of how to analyze financial data programmatically, a valuable skill set for data-driven decision-making.
crumbs: Get Financial data for data analysis

title: BCG_GenAI_The_Forage
text: This file will become your README and also the index of your documentation.

title: BCG_GenAI_The_Forage
text:
Developer Guide
If you are new to using nbdev here are some useful pointers to get you started.

Install BCG_GenAI_The_Forage in Development mode
# make sure BCG_GenAI_The_Forage package is installed in development mode
$ pip install -e .

# make changes under nbs/ directory
# ...

# compile to have changes apply to BCG_GenAI_The_Forage
$ nbdev_prepare

title: BCG_GenAI_The_Forage
text:
Usage

Installation
Install latest from the GitHub repository:
$ pip install git+https://github.com/Ramon-PR/BCG_GenAI_The_Forage.git
or from conda
$ conda install -c Ramon-PR BCG_GenAI_The_Forage
or from pypi
$ pip install BCG_GenAI_The_Forage


Documentation
Documentation can be found hosted on this GitHub repository’s pages. Additionally you can find package manager specific guidelines on conda and pypi respectively.

title: BCG_GenAI_The_Forage
text:
How to use
Fill me in please! Don’t forget code examples:

1+1

section: Percentage of change of each financial fact
text:
Percentage of change of each financial fact
Order by company and ascending year, for tools like .pct_change() whose operations operates in rows such as:
> \((row_{i+1}- row_{i}) / row_{i}\)

The percentage change has to be applied to all the facts (all columns except Company and Year)

cols_to_change = df.columns.difference(['Company', 'Year'])
cols_to_change

Index(['Cash Flow from Operations', 'Net Income', 'Total Assets',
       'Total Liabilities', 'Total Revenue'],
      dtype='object')


To avoid applying the method co rows of different companies, groupby Company

pct_change_df = df.groupby("Company")[cols_to_change].pct_change() * 100
# Add suffix to the new columns to indicate they are growth percentages
pct_change_df = pct_change_df.add_suffix(' growth (%)')

title: Load data / Manually input the data
section: Fill NA values that result from pct_change calculations with 0 or an appropriate value
crumbs: Load data / Manually input the data

title: Load data / Manually input the data
section: Optionally, you could summarize these findings for each company
text:
Optionally, you could summarize these findings for each company

df_with_growth.columns

Index(['Company', 'Year', 'Total Revenue', 'Net Income', 'Total Assets',
       'Total Liabilities', 'Cash Flow from Operations',
       'Cash Flow from Operations growth (%)', 'Net Income growth (%)',
       'Total Assets growth (%)', 'Total Liabilities growth (%)',
       'Total Revenue growth (%)'],
      dtype='object')



summary = df_with_growth.groupby('Company').agg({
    'Total Revenue growth (%)': 'mean',
    'Net Income growth (%)': 'mean'
}).reset_index()

print("\nYear-over-Year Average Growth Rates (%):")
print(summary)


Year-over-Year Average Growth Rates (%):
     Company  Total Revenue growth (%)  Net Income growth (%)
0      Apple                 -0.259489              -2.057837
1  Microsoft                 10.200706              12.446679
2      Tesla                  6.580948             -11.088841
crumbs: Load data / Manually input the data

title: Load data / Manually input the data
section: Plot the percentage of change of the financial facts accross years
text:
Plot the percentage of change of the financial facts accross years

fig, axs = plt.subplots(3,2, figsize=(10, 6))

for col in cols_to_change:
    ax = axs.flatten()[cols_to_change.get_loc(col)]
    sns.barplot(data=df_with_growth, x="Year", y=f"{col} growth (%)", hue="Company", ax=ax)
    ax.legend(loc="upper left")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()









2023 was not a good financial year and companies like Apple and Tesla have negative growth in their Cash Flow from operations, and even in 2024 the consecuences of 2023 can be seen in the Net income growth and Total Revenue growth.
Both Tesla and Apple have their main business related to selling physical products, however Microsoft is specialised in software and their growth is stable for the current data.
crumbs: Load data / Manually input the data

title: Getting the data through scraping
text:
Initiate a conda repo

conda create -n my_env

Install the Python library to scrape data from SEC Edgar API

uv add edgartools

Import package
Set your identity

set_identity("Ramon Pozuelo rapozueloruiz@gmail.com")

Now you can import Companies

apple = Company("AAPL")
# apple

Get the filing that we want (10 K of the previous year)

fillings = apple.get_filings(form="10-K", year=2024)
# fillings = apple.get_filings(form="10-K").latest()

Access the filing data

apple_latest_10K = fillings.latest()
apple_latest_10K

To donwload the filing content:

tenk = apple_latest_10K.obj()
tenk

The relevant information for the accounting is in : + Item 7 Management’s Discussion and Analysis (MD&A)

tenk["Item 7"]


apple.balance_sheet()


microsoft = Company("MSFT")
microsoft.balance_sheet(as_dataframe=True)


df = microsoft.balance_sheet(as_dataframe=True)


df.head()
